sentences.chinese: 卷五十 張馮汲鄭傳第二十
sentences.literal: Volume 50, twentieth: Biographies of Zhang, Feng, Ji, and Zheng
sentences.idiomatic: Volume 50: The Biographies of Zhang, Feng, Ji, and Zheng (No. 20).

sentences.chinese: 張釋之
sentences.literal: Zhang Shizhi
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Shizhi

sentences.chinese: 張釋之字季，南陽堵陽人也。
sentences.literal: Zhang Shizhi, courtesy name Ji, was a native of Duyang in Nanyang commandery.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Shizhi, whose courtesy name was Ji, came from Duyang in Nanyang.

sentences.chinese: 與兄仲同居，以貲為騎郎，事文帝，十年不得調，亡所知名。
sentences.literal: He dwelt together with his elder brother Zhong; by paying a fee he became a mounted attendant; he served Emperor Wen for ten years without being transferred or promoted, and had no reputation whatever.
sentences.idiomatic: He lived with his older brother Zhong, bought his way into the post of mounted attendant, and attended Emperor Wen; for ten years he was never moved to a new post and remained entirely unknown.

sentences.chinese: 釋之曰：「久宦減仲之產，不遂。」
sentences.literal: Shizhi said, "Long serving as an official has reduced Zhong's property; it has not succeeded."
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi said, "My long service as an official has eaten away at Zhong's estate; nothing has come of it."

sentences.chinese: 欲免歸。
sentences.literal: He wished to resign and return home.
sentences.idiomatic: He wanted to resign and go home.

sentences.chinese: 中郎將爰盎知其賢，惜其去，乃請徙釋之補謁者。
sentences.literal: The general of gentlemen-at-arms Yuan Ang knew he was worthy, regretted that he would leave, and therefore asked to have Shizhi transferred to fill a vacancy as usher.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang, general of the gentlemen-at-arms, knew Shizhi was capable and hated to lose him, so he asked that Shizhi be reassigned to serve as an usher.

sentences.chinese: 釋之既朝畢，因前言便宜事。
sentences.literal: When Shizhi had finished his court audience, he took the opportunity to speak first of what was expedient.
sentences.idiomatic: After court was dismissed, Shizhi stepped forward and spoke of policies that would benefit the state.

sentences.chinese: 文帝曰：「卑之，毋甚高論，令今可行也。」
sentences.literal: The emperor said, "Keep it modest; do not launch into lofty theories; let what you say be feasible in the present."
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Wen said, "Keep it practical; spare me lofty theory—tell me what can actually be done today."

sentences.chinese: 於是釋之言秦、漢之間事，秦所以失，漢所以興者。
sentences.literal: Thereupon Shizhi spoke of affairs between Qin and Han—why Qin lost the realm and why Han rose.
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi then traced events from Qin through to Han, explaining how Qin had lost the empire and how Han had gained it.

sentences.chinese: 文帝稱善，拜釋之為謁者僕射。
sentences.literal: The emperor praised him and appointed Shizhi chief usher.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor approved, and Shizhi was appointed chief usher (pushe).

sentences.chinese: 從行，上登虎圈，問上林尉禽獸簿，十餘問，尉左右視，盡不能對。
sentences.literal: On an outing the sovereign ascended the tiger enclosure and questioned the commandant of Shanglin about the register of birds and beasts with more than ten questions; the commandant looked left and right, and none could answer.
sentences.idiomatic: During a progress the emperor climbed to the tiger park and put more than ten questions to the Shanglin commandant about the game register; the man looked from side to side and could answer none of them.

sentences.chinese: 虎圈嗇夫從旁代尉對上所問禽獸簿甚悉，欲以觀其能口對向應亡窮者。
sentences.literal: The overseer of the tiger enclosure, standing by, answered for the commandant everything the sovereign had asked about the register of birds and beasts in great detail, wishing thereby to display one who could answer orally on the spot without limit.
sentences.idiomatic: The park overseer at the tiger pen, standing nearby, answered every question about the register in exhaustive detail, hoping to show off a man who could talk his way through anything.

sentences.chinese: 文帝曰：「吏不當如此邪？
sentences.literal: The emperor said, "Should not officials be like this?"
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Is this not how an official ought to behave?"

sentences.chinese: 尉亡賴！」
sentences.literal: The commandant is worthless!"
sentences.idiomatic: "The commandant is useless!"

sentences.chinese: 詔釋之拜嗇夫為上林令。
sentences.literal: He ordered Shizhi to appoint the overseer as superintendent of Shanglin.
sentences.idiomatic: He told Shizhi to promote that overseer to superintendent of Shanglin.

sentences.chinese: 釋之前曰：「陛下以絳侯周勃何如人也？」
sentences.literal: Shizhi stepped forward and said, "Your Majesty, what sort of man do you consider Marquis of Jiang Zhou Bo to be?"
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi stepped forward: "Your Majesty, what manner of man do you take Marquis of Jiang Zhou Bo to be?"

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「上者。」
sentences.literal: The sovereign said, "A superior man."
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor said, "He is a man of substance—a steady elder in conduct."

sentences.chinese: 又復問：「東陽侯張相如何如人也？」
sentences.literal: He asked again, "What sort of man do you consider Marquis of Dongyang Zhang Xiangru to be?"
sentences.idiomatic: He asked again: "And what of Marquis of Dongyang Zhang Xiangru?"

sentences.chinese: 上復曰：「長者。」
sentences.literal: The sovereign again said, "A senior worthy."
sentences.idiomatic: Again the emperor said, "The same—a steady, venerable sort of man."

sentences.chinese: 釋之曰：「夫絳侯、東陽侯稱為長者，此兩人言事曾不能出口，豈效此嗇夫喋喋利口捷給哉！
sentences.literal: Shizhi said, "When Marquis of Jiang and Marquis of Dongyang are called senior worthies, those two could not even get a word out when discussing affairs—are they to be imitated by this overseer chattering with a sharp tongue and quick answers?
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi replied, "Those two are honored as elder statesmen, yet neither could get a sentence out when state business was discussed. Is Your Majesty to take this overseer, with his endless patter and glib tongue, as the model?"

sentences.chinese: 且秦以任刀筆之吏，爭以亟疾苛察相高，其敝徒文具，亡惻隱之實。
sentences.literal: Moreover Qin, by entrusting power to brush-and-knife clerks, made them compete to outdo one another in harsh severity and rapid scrutiny; its harm was mere paperwork without the reality of compassion.
sentences.idiomatic: Besides, the Qin put petty clerks in charge; they vied to be harsher and quicker to find fault, until government was nothing but paper and the human touch vanished.

sentences.chinese: 以故不聞其過，陵夷至於二世，天下土崩。
sentences.literal: For that reason the ruler did not hear of his faults, and decline stretched to the Second Generation until all under heaven crumbled.
sentences.idiomatic: The ruler therefore never heard his own faults; decline ran on to the Second Emperor, and the realm fell apart.

sentences.chinese: 今陛下以嗇夫口辯而超遷之，臣恐天下隨風靡，爭口辯，亡其實。
sentences.literal: Now if Your Majesty, because the overseer is eloquent with his mouth, promotes him precipitously, I fear all under heaven will follow the wind and lean that way, striving for glibness and abandoning substance.
sentences.idiomatic: If you now leap this man ahead because he talks well, I fear the whole country will take its cue, chase clever words, and forget what is real.

sentences.chinese: 且下之化上，疾於景□，舉錯不可不察也。」
sentences.literal: Moreover when inferiors take their model from superiors it is swifter than shadow and echo; what you raise up and set down cannot go unexamined."
sentences.idiomatic: What those below learn from those above spreads faster than shadow or echo; every move you make in appointments must be weighed with care."

sentences.chinese: 文帝曰：「善。」
sentences.literal: The emperor said, "Good."
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Well said."

sentences.chinese: 乃止不拜嗇夫。
sentences.literal: He therefore desisted and did not appoint the overseer.
sentences.idiomatic: He dropped the idea and never gave the overseer the post.

sentences.chinese: 就車，召釋之驂乘，徐行，行問釋之秦之敝。
sentences.literal: [The emperor] entered his carriage, summoned Shizhi to ride escort beside him, proceeded slowly, and as they went questioned Shizhi about the failings of Qin.
sentences.idiomatic: He got into his carriage, had Shizhi take the place beside the driver, and drove slowly, asking as they went what had gone wrong under the Qin.

sentences.chinese: 具以質言。
sentences.literal: Shizhi reported everything with blunt, substantive answers.
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi answered plainly and to the point.

sentences.chinese: 至宮，上拜釋之為公車令。
sentences.literal: When they reached the palace, the sovereign appointed Shizhi marshal of the imperial carriage.
sentences.idiomatic: Back at the palace the emperor appointed him marshal of the imperial carriage.

sentences.chinese: 頃之，太子與梁王共車入朝，不下司馬門，於是釋之追止太子、梁王毋入殿門。
sentences.literal: Before long, the heir apparent and the king of Liang shared a carriage into court; they did not dismount at the Sima gate. Shizhi therefore ran after them and stopped the heir apparent and the king of Liang from entering the hall gate.
sentences.idiomatic: Soon after, the crown prince and the king of Liang rode together into the palace precincts without alighting at the Sima Gate; Shizhi overtook them and barred them from the inner hall until they complied.

sentences.chinese: 遂劾不下公門不敬，奏之。
sentences.literal: He thereupon impeached them for failing to dismount at the public gate as an act of disrespect and memorialized it.
sentences.idiomatic: He then filed a charge of disrespect for not dismounting at the outer gate and submitted it to the throne.

sentences.chinese: 薄太后聞之，文帝免冠謝曰：「教兒子不謹。」
sentences.literal: When Empress Dowager Bo heard of it, Emperor Wen removed his cap and apologized, saying, "I failed to instruct my sons with strictness."
sentences.idiomatic: When Empress Dowager Bo learned of it, Emperor Wen doffed his cap and apologized: "I was remiss in teaching my sons."

sentences.chinese: 薄太后使使承詔赦太子、梁王，然後得入。
sentences.literal: Empress Dowager Bo sent an envoy bearing an edict pardoning the crown prince and the king of Liang; only then could they enter.
sentences.idiomatic: The empress dowager sent messengers with an edict pardoning the crown prince and the king of Liang, after which they were allowed in.

sentences.chinese: 文帝繇是奇釋之，拜為中大夫。
sentences.literal: From this Emperor Wen thought Shizhi extraordinary and appointed him grand counsellor of the palace.
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Wen was struck by Shizhi from that day on and promoted him to grand counsellor of the palace.

sentences.chinese: 頃之，至中郎將。
sentences.literal: Before long he rose to general of gentlemen-at-arms.
sentences.idiomatic: In due course he rose to general of the gentlemen-at-arms.

sentences.chinese: 從行至霸陵，上居外臨廁。
sentences.literal: On progress they reached Baling; the sovereign stopped outside and leaned over the privy.
sentences.idiomatic: On a progress to Baling the emperor halted outside and stepped to the privy.

sentences.chinese: 時慎夫人從，上指視慎夫人新豐道，曰：「此走邯鄲道也。」
sentences.literal: At that time Lady Shen was in attendance; the sovereign pointed out to Lady Shen the road to Xinfeng and said, "This is the road that runs to Handan."
sentences.idiomatic: Lady Shen was with him; he pointed toward the Xinfeng road for her and said, "That is the road to Handan."

sentences.chinese: 使慎夫人鼓瑟，上自倚瑟而歌，意淒愴悲懷，顧謂群臣曰：「嗟乎！
sentences.literal: He had Lady Shen strum the se; the sovereign himself leaned on the se and sang, his mood mournful and sorrowful, and turning to the ministers he said, "Alas!
sentences.idiomatic: He had Lady Shen play the se and himself sang along, leaning on the instrument, his heart full of grief; turning to the ministers he said, "Ah,

sentences.chinese: 以北山石為槨，用□絮斫陳漆其間，豈可動哉！」
sentences.literal: If we take the stone north of the mountain for the outer coffin, and pack floss in layers and lacquer the gaps between, could anything disturb it!"
sentences.idiomatic: if we used the northern mountain for an outer shell and filled the joints with packed floss and lacquer, who could ever breach it!"

sentences.chinese: 左右皆曰：「善。」
sentences.literal: Those on his left and right all said, "Good."
sentences.idiomatic: Those at his side said, "Indeed."

sentences.chinese: 釋之前曰：「使其中有可欲，雖錮南山猶有隙；
sentences.literal: Shizhi stepped forward and said, "If there is still within it something people might covet, though you seal off the South Mountain there would still be a crack;
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi stepped forward: "If there is anything inside worth stealing, sealing it with the whole Southern Range would still leave a crack;

sentences.chinese: 使其中亡可欲，雖亡石槨，又何戚焉？」
sentences.literal: if there is nothing within worth coveting, even without a stone outer coffin, what grief would there be?"
sentences.idiomatic: if there is nothing worth taking, then even without a stone sarcophagus, what is there to fear?"

sentences.chinese: 文帝稱善。
sentences.literal: The emperor praised him.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor praised his answer.

sentences.chinese: 其後，拜釋之為廷尉。
sentences.literal: After that he appointed Shizhi commandant of justice.
sentences.idiomatic: He later appointed Shizhi commandant of justice (tingwei).

sentences.chinese: 頃之，上行出中渭橋，有一人從橋下走，乘輿馬驚。
sentences.literal: Before long, on a progress the sovereign was leaving by the Zhongwei Bridge when a man ran from under the bridge and startled the horses of the imperial equipage.
sentences.idiomatic: Not long after, the emperor was crossing the middle Wei Bridge when someone darted from beneath it and spooked the imperial horses.

sentences.chinese: 於是使騎捕之，屬廷尉。
sentences.literal: Thereupon he had horsemen seize him and turn him over to the commandant of justice.
sentences.idiomatic: Horsemen were sent to seize the man and hand him to the commandant of justice.

sentences.chinese: 釋之治問。
sentences.literal: Shizhi tried and questioned him.
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi examined him under the law.

sentences.chinese: 曰：「縣人來，聞蹕，匿橋下。
sentences.literal: He said, "I am a man of the county who came; hearing the clearing of the way I hid under the bridge.
sentences.idiomatic: The man said, "I am a commoner from the countryside. I heard the procession clearing the road and hid under the bridge."

sentences.chinese: 久，以為行過，既出，見車騎，即走耳。」
sentences.literal: After a long time I thought the procession had passed; when I came out and saw the chariots and riders I ran—that is all."
sentences.idiomatic: I waited so long I thought the train had gone; when I stepped out and saw the escort I ran—that is all there is to it."

sentences.chinese: 釋之奏當：「此人犯蹕，當罰金。」
sentences.literal: Shizhi reported the appropriate verdict: "This person violated the clearing; the penalty is a fine in gold."
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi reported the statutory sentence: "This man broke the law on clearing the way; the fine is in gold."

sentences.chinese: 上怒曰：「此人親驚吾馬，馬賴和柔，令它馬，固不敗傷我乎？
sentences.literal: The emperor angrily said, "He personally startled my horses; thanks to their mild temper, had it been other horses would they not surely have injured me?
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor flared: "He nearly threw my horses; they were gentle—another team might have killed me."

sentences.chinese: 而廷尉乃當之罰金！」
sentences.literal: Yet the commandant would match that with a fine in gold!"
sentences.idiomatic: And you answer with a mere fine!"

sentences.chinese: 釋之曰：「法者，天子所與天下公共也。
sentences.literal: Shizhi said, "Law is what the Son of Heaven shares in common with all under heaven.
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi said, "The law is something Your Majesty holds in common with every subject."

sentences.chinese: 今法如是，更重之，是法不信於民也。
sentences.literal: Now the law is thus; to make it heavier would be for the law not to be trusted by the people.
sentences.idiomatic: The statute says what it says; to stretch it heavier would teach the people not to trust the law.

sentences.chinese: 且方其時，上使使誅之則已。
sentences.literal: Moreover at the moment had Your Majesty sent someone to execute him it would have been over.
sentences.idiomatic: Besides, at the moment it happened you could have sent a man to behead him and been done.

sentences.chinese: 今已下廷尉，廷尉，天下之平也，壹傾，天下用法皆為之輕重，民安所錯其手足？
sentences.literal: Now that it has come down to the commandant of justice—the commandant of justice is the balance for all the world—once it tips one way, everywhere under heaven application of the law would grow light or heavy accordingly; where are the people to place their hands and feet?
sentences.idiomatic: Now it has reached the commandant of justice—and he is the level for the whole realm. Tip that balance once, and every magistrate will bend sentences up or down; where are ordinary folk to put their hands and feet?

sentences.chinese: 唯陛下察之。」
sentences.literal: I beg Your Majesty to examine this."
sentences.idiomatic: I beg you to think this through."

sentences.chinese: 上良久曰：「廷尉當是也。」
sentences.literal: After a long silence the sovereign said, "The commandant's verdict is correct."
sentences.idiomatic: After a long silence the emperor said, "The commandant is right."

sentences.chinese: 其後人有盜高廟座前玉環，得，文帝怒，下廷尉治。
sentences.literal: Later someone stole a jade ring from before the seat in Gaozu's temple; he was caught and Emperor Wen, furious, handed him to the commandant of justice for trial.
sentences.idiomatic: Later someone stole a jade ring from the dais in Gaozu's temple; when he was caught Emperor Wen, in a rage, sent him to the commandant of justice.

sentences.chinese: 案盜宗廟服御物者為奏，當棄市。
sentences.literal: When the case of one who stole ritual objects from the ancestral temple was reported, the matched penalty was public execution in the market.
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi judged theft of imperial ritual gear from the ancestral shrines and recommended execution in the marketplace.

sentences.chinese: 上大怒曰：「人亡道，乃盜先帝器！
sentences.literal: The emperor said in great anger, "That man lacks the way; he stole the late emperor's vessel!
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor thundered, "The man is utterly depraved—he stole an object of the late emperor!

sentences.chinese: 吾屬廷尉者，欲致之族，而君以法奏之，非吾所以共承宗廟意也。」
sentences.literal: I handed him to the commandant of justice wishing to have his kin exterminated; yet you memorialized according to the law—that is not how I meant to share in sustaining the ancestral temple."
sentences.idiomatic: I sent him to you expecting the extermination of his clan; instead you cite the statutes. That is not how I meant to uphold the shrines of our house."

sentences.chinese: 釋之免冠頓首謝曰：「法如是足也。
sentences.literal: Shizhi removed his cap and knocked his head in apology, saying, "According to the law this is sufficient punishment.
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi doffed his cap and kowtowed: "The law already prescribes the utmost for this offense.

sentences.chinese: 且罪等，然以逆順為基。
sentences.literal: Moreover when crimes are equal, the judgment takes orderly versus rebellious conduct as its basis.
sentences.idiomatic: When crimes are of the same grade, one still weighs whether the act was calculated rebellion or mere disorder.

sentences.chinese: 今盜宗廟器而族之，有如萬分一，假令愚民取長陵一□土，陛下且何以加其法乎？」
sentences.literal: Now to exterminate a whole clan for stealing temple vessels—if there were one chance in ten thousand, suppose common folk took one handful of earth from Changling—how would Your Majesty add to the law then?"
sentences.idiomatic: To wipe out a clan for stealing from the temple—if there were the slightest chance some fool took a handful of soil from your own Changling mound, what heavier penalty could you add?

sentences.chinese: 文帝與太后言之，乃許廷尉當。
sentences.literal: The emperor spoke of it with the empress dowager and then approved the commandant's verdict.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor took the matter to the empress dowager and then accepted the commandant's sentence.

sentences.chinese: 是時，中尉條侯周亞夫與梁相山都侯王恬啟見釋之持議平，乃結為親友。
sentences.literal: At that time Commandant Zhou Yafu of the Tiao marquisate and Wang Tianqi, marquis of Shandu and minister of Liang, saw that Shizhi held opinions fairly; they therefore formed friendly ties with him.
sentences.idiomatic: At the time Commandant Zhou Yafu of the Tiao marquisate and Wang Tianqi, marquis of Shandu and minister of Liang, saw how fairly Shizhi decided cases and became his close friends.

sentences.chinese: 張廷尉繇此天下稱之。
sentences.literal: Commandant Zhang was praised throughout the realm for this.
sentences.idiomatic: From then on Commandant Zhang was honored across the empire.

sentences.chinese: 文帝崩，景帝立，釋之恐，稱疾。
sentences.literal: When Emperor Wen died and Emperor Jing took the throne, Shizhi was afraid and pleaded illness.
sentences.idiomatic: When Wen died and Jing succeeded, Shizhi grew fearful and pleaded illness.

sentences.chinese: 欲免去，懼大誅至；
sentences.literal: He wished to resign and leave but feared execution on a great scale would come;
sentences.idiomatic: He wanted to resign yet dreaded a sweeping punishment;

sentences.chinese: 欲見，則未知何如。
sentences.literal: he wished to appear but did not know how it would go.
sentences.idiomatic: he thought of facing the new emperor but could not tell how he would be received.

sentences.chinese: 用王生計，卒見謝，景帝不過也。
sentences.literal: Following Wang Sheng's plan he finally went in to apologize, and Emperor Jing did not fault him beyond measure.
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Sheng's advice won out: he went in to apologize, and Emperor Jing did not press the old grudge too far.

sentences.chinese: 王生者，善為黃、老言，處士。
sentences.literal: Wang Sheng was skilled at discourse in the Huang-Lao tradition and was a recluse.
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Sheng was a recluse who excelled at Huang-Lao teaching.

sentences.chinese: 嘗召居廷中，公卿盡會立。
sentences.literal: He was once summoned to reside in the palace when the dukes and ministers had all met and stood in order.
sentences.idiomatic: Once he was called to wait inside the palace while the high ministers stood assembled.

sentences.chinese: 王生老人，曰「吾襪解」，顧謂釋之：「為我結襪！」
sentences.literal: Wang Sheng, an old man, said "My leg bands have come loose," and looking toward Shizhi said, "Tie my leg bands for me!"
sentences.idiomatic: This old man suddenly said, "My leggings have slipped," and told Shizhi, "Tie them for me!"

sentences.chinese: 釋之跪而結之，既已，人或讓王生：「獨奈何廷辱張廷尉如此？」
sentences.literal: Shizhi knelt and tied them; when it was done someone reproached Wang Sheng: "How could you alone thus humiliate Commandant Zhang in court?"
sentences.idiomatic: Shizhi knelt and fastened them. Afterwards someone scolded Wang Sheng: "Why humiliate Commandant Zhang like that in open court?"

sentences.chinese: 王生曰：「吾老且賤，自度終亡益於張廷尉。
sentences.literal: Wang Sheng said, "I am old and of lowly station; I reckon I can never benefit Commandant Zhang in the end.
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Sheng said, "I am old and obscure; I will never advance Zhang's career by my own weight.

sentences.chinese: 廷尉方天下名臣，吾故聊使結襪，欲以重之。」
sentences.literal: The commandant of justice is the empire's famed minister; I deliberately had him tie my leg bands for a moment wishing thereby to give him added weight."
sentences.idiomatic: He is already the realm's most celebrated judge; I meant only to lend him a little extra dignity by having him do this."

sentences.chinese: 諸公聞之，賢王生而重釋之。
sentences.literal: When the dukes heard this they thought Wang Sheng worthy and held Shizhi in higher regard.
sentences.idiomatic: The nobles heard the story, admired Wang Sheng's tact, and respected Shizhi all the more.

sentences.chinese: 釋之事景帝歲餘，為淮南相，猶尚以前過也。
sentences.literal: Shizhi served Emperor Jing for over a year as chancellor of Huainan, still on account of the earlier offense.
sentences.idiomatic: After a little more than a year serving Jing, Shizhi was made chancellor of Huainan—still a demotion tied to the earlier incident at the gate.

sentences.chinese: 年老病卒。
sentences.literal: He died of old age and illness.
sentences.idiomatic: He died in old age of natural causes.

sentences.chinese: 其子摯，字長公，官至大夫，免。
sentences.literal: His son Zhi, courtesy name Zhangong, rose to the rank of grandee but was dismissed.
sentences.idiomatic: His son Zhi, courtesy name Zhangong, reached the rank of grandee before being dismissed.

sentences.chinese: 以不能取容當世，故終身不仕。
sentences.literal: Because he could not accommodate himself to the age he never served in office all his life.
sentences.idiomatic: Unable to trim his sails to the times, he never held office again.

sentences.chinese: 馮唐，祖父趙人也。
sentences.literal: Feng Tang; his grandfather was a man of Zhao.
sentences.idiomatic: Feng Tang's grandfather was a native of Zhao.

sentences.chinese: 父徙代。
sentences.literal: His father moved to Dai.
sentences.idiomatic: His father later resettled in Dai.

sentences.chinese: 漢興徙安陵。
sentences.literal: When Han rose they moved to Anling.
sentences.idiomatic: After the Han founding the family moved to Anling.

sentences.chinese: 唐以孝著，為郎中署長，事文帝。
sentences.literal: Tang was noted for filial piety; he was chief of a section in the office of palace gentlemen and served Emperor Wen.
sentences.idiomatic: Feng Tang was known for filial devotion; he headed a desk in the gentlemen's office and served under Emperor Wen.

sentences.chinese: 帝輦過，問唐曰：「父老何自為郎？
sentences.literal: When the imperial palanquin passed by, [the emperor] asked Tang, "Elder, how did you yourself become a gentleman-at-court?"
sentences.idiomatic: As the imperial litter passed, the emperor called out, "Old sir, how is it that you yourself hold the post of gentleman-at-court?"

sentences.chinese: 家安在？」
sentences.literal: "Where is your home?"
sentences.idiomatic: "Where is your household?"

sentences.chinese: 具以實言。
sentences.literal: Tang answered with the full truth.
sentences.idiomatic: Feng Tang told him the whole story plainly.

sentences.chinese: 文帝曰：「吾居代時，吾尚食監高祛數為我言趙將李齊之賢，戰於鉅鹿下。
sentences.literal: Emperor Wen said, "When I dwelt in Dai my commissioner of palace provender Gao Qu often spoke to me of the worth of Zhao's general Li Qi and how he fought below Julu.
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Wen said, "In my days in Dai my palace provender Gao Qu used to praise Zhao's general Li Qi and his fighting below Julu."

sentences.chinese: 吾每飲食，意未嘗不在鉅鹿也。
sentences.literal: Whenever I eat and drink my thoughts have never not been at Julu.
sentences.idiomatic: Whenever I dine, my mind is still there at Julu.

sentences.chinese: 父老知之乎？」
sentences.literal: Do you know of that, elder?"
sentences.idiomatic: Do you know that story, old sir?"

sentences.chinese: 唐對曰：「齊尚不如廉頗、李牧之為將也。」
sentences.literal: Tang replied, "As a general Li Qi cannot yet match Lian Po or Li Mu."
sentences.idiomatic: Feng Tang answered, "Li Qi was no match, as a commander, for Lian Po or Li Mu."

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「何已？」
sentences.literal: The sovereign said, "What of it?"
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor said, "How so?"

sentences.chinese: 唐曰：「臣大父在趙時，為官帥將，善李牧。
sentences.literal: Tang said, "When my grandfather was in Zhao he led troops as a commandery officer and was close to Li Mu.
sentences.idiomatic: Feng Tang said, "My grandfather served in Zhao as a commandery officer commanding troops and was on close terms with Li Mu.

sentences.chinese: 臣父故為代相，善李齊，知其為人也。」
sentences.literal: My late father formerly was chancellor of Dai and was close to Li Qi; I know what kind of man he was."
sentences.idiomatic: My late father was chancellor of Dai and knew Li Qi well; I understand what sort of general he was."

sentences.chinese: 上既聞廉頗、李牧為人，良說，乃拊髀曰：「嗟乎！
sentences.literal: Having heard how Lian Po and Li Mu were as men, [the emperor] was greatly pleased and slapped his thigh, saying, "Alas!
sentences.idiomatic: Learning what manner of men Lian Po and Li Mu had been, the emperor was delighted; he slapped his thigh and cried, "Ah,

sentences.chinese: 吾獨不得廉頗、李牧為將，豈憂匈奴哉！」
sentences.literal: If only I could obtain Lian Po and Li Mu as generals, would I need to worry about the Xiongnu!"
sentences.idiomatic: if only I had Lian Po and Li Mu to lead my armies—would I still lose sleep over the Xiongnu!"
